content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA of length NNN. Your goal is to make all elements of AAA distinct. To achieve this, you can perform the following operation any number of times:


Select two indices lll and rrr such that  1≤l≤r≤∣A∣1 ≤ l ≤ r ≤ |A|1≤l≤r≤∣A∣. In the subarray  A[l…r]A [ l … r ]A[l…r], keep only the first occurrence of each element, and remove all subsequent repetitions of each element within this range.
The cost of performing this operation is number of distinct elements in initial subarray A[l…r]A [ l … r ]A[l…r] before doing this operation.

Your task is to calculate the **minimum** total cost required to make all elements of the final array AAA distinct.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains NNN — the number of elements in AAA.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​, denoting the array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the minimum total cost required to make all elements of the final array AAA distinct.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 222: We can perform the following operation:

[2,2‾]→L=1,R=2[2][\underline{2, 2}] \xrightarrow{L = 1, R = 2} [2][2,2​]L=1,R=2​[2] with cost 111.

Test case 333: All the elements of AAA are already distinct.
Test case 444: One possible sequence of operations is as follows:


[1,1,2,3,3,2‾]→L=3,R=6[1,1,2,3][1, 1, \underline{2, 3, 3, 2}] \xrightarrow{L = 3, R = 6} [1, 1, 2, 3][1,1,2,3,3,2​]L=3,R=6​[1,1,2,3] with cost 222.


[1,1‾,2,3]→L=1,R=2[1,2,3][\underline{1, 1}, 2, 3] \xrightarrow{L = 1, R = 2} [1, 2, 3][1,1​,2,3]L=1,R=2​[1,2,3] with cost 111.


Total cost will be 333. It can be shown that it's impossible to make all elements of this array distinct with less than total cost of 333.
